title: Dog Red Jelly-Like Stool: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is passing red jelly-like stool, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn about AHDS and why immediate vet care is required.
content_html:
TL;DR: Red jelly-like stool is a high-urgency veterinary emergency often caused by Acute Hemorrhagic Diarrhea Syndrome (AHDS). You must seek immediate veterinary care to prevent life-threatening dehydration and shock.

What does it mean if my dog has red, jelly-like stool?

Finding stool that resembles strawberry jam or red jelly is a sign that your dog is experiencing significant inflammation and bleeding in the lower intestinal tract. This appearance is caused by a mixture of blood, mucus, and the sloughing of the intestinal lining. It is most commonly associated with a condition called Acute Hemorrhagic Diarrhea Syndrome (AHDS), previously known as HGE.

Is red jelly-like stool in dogs a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Yes, this is a critical veterinary emergency.
  • Unlike standard diarrhea, AHDS causes a dog to lose blood and fluids at an alarmingly fast rate.
  • This condition can lead to life-threatening dehydration, electrolyte imbalances, and hypovolemic shock within hours.
  • If you see red jelly-like stool, do not wait; contact your veterinarian or the nearest emergency animal hospital immediately.

Why should I take a photo of my dog's bloody stool for the vet?

  • A clear photo of the red jelly-like stool serves as an invaluable diagnostic tool during triage.
  • It allows the triage technician or veterinarian to immediately assess the volume and nature of the blood loss.
  • This helps the medical team prioritize your dog’s care the moment you arrive and can speed up the start of life-saving treatments like intravenous fluid therapy.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

An acute onset of profuse hemorrhagic diarrhea, often described as resembling raspberry jam, is characteristic of hemorrhagic gastroenteritis (HGE), particularly in small or toy breed dogs. Additional clinical signs include vomiting, anorexia, lethargy, and abdominal pain. Rapid fluid loss can lead to hypovolemic shock, potentially preceding recognizable dehydration. Diagnosis is typically based on signalment, acute clinical signs, hemoconcentration (PCV ≥ 55%), and normal to slightly decreased total plasma protein concentration. While selective fecal cultures for pathogens like Clostridium spp, Salmonella spp, Yersinia spp, and enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli, as well as evaluation for Clostridium spp enterotoxin via fecal ELISA, may be considered, the primary concern involves addressing hypovolemic shock and potential sepsis.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 396)

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning if my dog seems okay otherwise?
faqs.answer: No. Dogs with AHDS can appear relatively normal initially but may crash and enter shock very quickly. Immediate medical intervention is necessary regardless of how 'fine' they seem.

faqs.question: What causes this condition?
faqs.answer: The exact cause of AHDS is often unknown, but it is frequently linked to dietary indiscretion (eating something they shouldn't), sudden stress, toxins, or an overgrowth of certain bacteria like Clostridium perfringens.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to help?
faqs.answer: Treatment usually involves aggressive intravenous (IV) fluid therapy to combat dehydration, along with anti-nausea medications and sometimes antibiotics or specialized diets to help the gut heal.
